---
title: "caladaptaer"
subtitle: "Cal-Adapt climate projections, straight into R"
---

If you work in R and you have tried to pull California's downscaled climate
projections off the [Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine](https://analytics.cal-adapt.org/),
you have probably hit a wall. The data sits as cloud optimized Zarr on AWS S3,
and until now the R side of that story basically did not exist. caladaptaer is
the piece that was missing. You ask for a variable, a model, a scenario, and a
time window, and it hands back a native R object, a `stars` grid or a tidy
`data.frame`, ready to drop into the rest of your work. No Python, no API key,
nothing to authenticate.

And it stays general on purpose. caladaptaer is a data gateway, not a model.
Whatever you do in R, ecology, hydrology, ag, energy, water resources, carbon,
you point it at the projection you need and keep working in your own tools.

## How it works

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart LR
  classDef data fill:#F6A23C,stroke:#C77B1E,color:#1f1300,stroke-width:1px;
  classDef fn fill:#BBD8EC,stroke:#3B7DB5,color:#0f3350,stroke-width:1px;
  classDef next fill:#EAF3FB,stroke:#3B7DB5,color:#0f3350,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 3;
  classDef goal fill:#ffffff,stroke:#555555,color:#111111,stroke-width:1px;

  subgraph DATA["Data, level by level"]
    direction TB
    S["Cal-Adapt on AWS S3<br/>WRF and LOCA2 Zarr stores"]:::data
    R["native R object<br/>stars grid or data.frame,<br/>one variable"]:::data
    A["analysis ready<br/>harmonized across<br/>WRF and LOCA2"]:::data
    D["model drivers<br/>CF NetCDF"]:::data
    G(["your science<br/>any domain"]):::goal
    S --> R --> A --> D --> G
    A --> G
  end

  subgraph CODE["caladaptaer"]
    direction TB
    DISC["Discover<br/>cae_catalog, cae_models, cae_scenarios,<br/>cae_variables, cae_search, cae_check_variables"]:::fn
    FETCH["Fetch, the engine<br/>cae_fetch, cae_fetch_point, cae_fetch_points"]:::fn
    HARM["Harmonize and stamp provenance<br/>next up"]:::next
    DRV["Drivers<br/>cae_write_cf_netcdf, cae_build_met_drivers"]:::fn
  end

  DISC -.->|"what is on S3"| S
  FETCH -->|"GDAL /vsis3/, no Python, no key, no auth"| R
  HARM --> A
  DRV --> D
```

The Analytics Engine stores its climate data as [Zarr](https://zarr.dev/), a
cloud native array format. A Zarr store is not one giant file you download
whole; it is split into many small chunks, and a reader can pull just the chunks
it needs over the network. caladaptaer reads those chunks through GDAL's
`/vsis3/` virtual filesystem, the same engine already sitting under `sf`,
`stars`, and `terra`. That is why there is nothing to install on the Python
side, no key to manage, and no client session to keep alive. You ask for what
you want and the bytes come back as an R object.

## The data you can reach

Two downscaling products live on the Engine, both downscaled from CMIP6 global
models for California and the western US.

| Dataset | Resolution | Step | GCMs | Scenarios | Period |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| WRF CMIP6 (WUS-D3) | 45 / 9 / 3 km | Hourly | 8 | SSP3-7.0 (all); SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 (CESM2) | 1980--2100 |
| LOCA2-Hybrid | 3 km | Daily | 15 | SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0, SSP5-8.5 | 1950--2100 |

They get there two different ways, and which one you want depends on the job.
WRF is dynamically downscaled by UCLA (Rahimi et al. 2024), a physics based
weather model run at high resolution, so it carries the full hourly surface
meteorology and resolves things like mountain rain shadows and the sea breeze.
Reach for it when you need hourly drivers or process based physics. LOCA2 is
statistically downscaled by Scripps (Pierce et al.), mapping coarse model output
onto a fine grid from historical analog patterns, so you get daily data for more
models and more scenarios at 3 km. Reach for it when you want a wide ensemble or
daily extremes. caladaptaer reads both, plus the ERA5 reanalysis driven WRF run,
through the same handful of functions.

## What's in the box

Eleven `cae_*` functions cover the whole path from catalog to model ready files.
You can search the CADCAT catalog by model, scenario, variable, and resolution,
and check up front whether a model and scenario even carry the fields you need.
You can read any Zarr store into a `stars` grid, or pull a time series at one
point or ten thousand; the multi site read grabs the grid from S3 once and
extracts every site locally, so it is one read per variable, not one read per
site. And you can turn WRF output into CF-1.8 NetCDF for ecosystem models, units
and all, in a single call. Precipitation gets filled in automatically for the
models that ship it split across `rainc` and `rainnc`.

## Quick start

```r
remotes::install_github("lebauerapproach/caladaptaer")
```

```r
library(caladaptaer)

# what is on the engine
cae_models(activity = "WRF")

# does CESM2 SSP3-7.0 carry the variables I need
cae_check_variables("CESM2", "ssp370")

# a summer day of hourly temperature at Fresno, 2050
cae_fetch_point("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                end_time   = "2050-07-01T23:00:00")
```

[Getting Started](vignettes/getting_started.qmd) takes this from install to a
real time series, [Data Access](vignettes/data_access.qmd) is the full tour of
every function with live output, and [Showcase](vignettes/showcase.qmd) answers
"what can I actually do with it" in a few worked analyses against live data.

## Used in production

We run caladaptaer in our MAGiC project to pull climate
projections for our California design points, where it has already generated
12,474 site-years of hourly drivers: three GCM and scenario combinations (CESM2
SSP2-4.5, CESM2 SSP3-7.0, MPI-ESM1-2-HR SSP3-7.0) over 2025 to 2045.

## Where we're headed

Today caladaptaer nails access: find the data, read it, turn it into model ready
files. Three things come next. First, an analysis ready layer that harmonizes
WRF and LOCA2 behind one interface and quietly applies Cal-Adapt's known data
quirks (the renamed humidity and wind variables, the precipitation unit fix), so
you write code once and run it on both. Second, provenance: every output stamped
with exactly where it came from, the store, version, slice, model, scenario, and
transform, plus the Cal-Adapt citation carried through, so you can rerun and
cite a file straight from itself. Third, the analysis layer that turns raw
projections into what people actually report on, spatial subsetting to counties
and watersheds, temporal aggregation, derived variables like growing degree days
and reference ET, warming levels, extremes, and bias correction. After that,
rOpenSci review and a CRAN submission.

Why it is worth doing: it is the only real path into the Analytics Engine for R
users right now, since the old R client stops at the previous CMIP5 generation.
The provenance piece is something even the Python tooling does not do today. And
it stays general, so the same gateway serves an ecologist, a hydrologist, or an
energy modeler. We are building it to sit next to the Python tools in the
Cal-Adapt ecosystem, not to fork them.

## Guides and reference

| Page | What is on it |
|---|---|
| [Getting Started](vignettes/getting_started.qmd) | Install to a real time series in a few calls |
| [Data Access](vignettes/data_access.qmd) | Every function, with live output |
| [Showcase](vignettes/showcase.qmd) | Worked analyses that show what the data can do |
| [SIPNET Met Drivers](vignettes/sipnet_met_drivers.qmd) | WRF to CF NetCDF for ecosystem models |
| [Bulk Met Drivers](vignettes/bulk_met_drivers.qmd) | The multi site driver path |
| [Reference](reference/index.html) | Every function, every argument |

## Citing and license

caladaptaer is released under BSD-3-Clause. See `CITATION.cff` in the repo for
how to cite it.
